findings.reachability: authenticated admin only — the AJAX handler enforces manage_options; the injected payload is rendered in the admin Activity tab DataTable
findings.justification: The `reason` column value fetched from `wpcatcha_accesslocks` is appended to the JSON output array as raw HTML (`$row[] = $aRow->$col`) with no escaping; the DataTable renders it as innerHTML, so a stored payload injected into the reason field (e.g. via a crafted lockdown trigger) executes in the admin browser.

findings.reachability: any visitor who reaches a page with the built-in captcha form; the payload is set by an admin via the captcha_challenge_text option
findings.justification: The admin-controlled option `captcha_challenge_text` is echoed directly into the public HTML label without any escaping: `$output .= '

findings.reachability: login page visitors; payload injected by an admin via design_background_image or design_form_background_image options
findings.justification: Both `design_background_image` and `design_form_background_image` are output inside CSS `url()` values using `esc_attr()` (e.g., `echo 'body.login {background-image:url(' . esc_attr($options['design_background_image']) . ')...'`), which does not prevent CSS injection; an attacker controlling the option value could break out of the url() context to inject arbitrary CSS rules.

findings.reachability: authenticated admin via the Activity tab AJAX call
findings.justification: When `log_passwords` is enabled, the `failed_pass` column — which stores actual attempted passwords submitted by users — is returned in the AJAX response and rendered in the admin DataTable, exposing potentially reused credentials to any admin with access to the Activity log view.
